    return lst

options: Type compatibility is determined by explicit inheritance declarations
Type compatibility is based on the structure or capabilities of a type, not its declared name
All types are treated as identical at runtime
Types are checked only at compile time

hard.questionText: What problem does the 'Two Generals Problem' illustrate in distributed systems?
options: The impossibility of leader election in async networks
The impossibility of achieving guaranteed consensus over an unreliable channel
The challenge of consistent hashing in distributed databases

hard.questionText: What is the key distinction between a 'coroutine' and a regular function?
options: Coroutines always run on separate threads
Coroutines can pause and resume execution, yielding control back to the caller
Coroutines are only available in Python

hard.questionText: What does it mean for a programming language to be 'Turing complete'?
options: It can simulate any Turing machine and thus compute any computable function
It has a built-in garbage collector
It supports both compiled and interpreted execution

hard.questionText: What is 'variance' in the context of generic type systems (covariance vs contravariance)?
options: The difference in execution speed between generic and non-generic code
How subtype relationships between complex types relate to subtype relationships of their component types
The number of type parameters a generic class can accept
Whether a generic type can be used at runtime or only at compile time

hard.questionText: In the Actor model of concurrency, how do actors communicate?
options: By passing immutable messages to each other's mailboxes
Through direct function calls on each other's methods

hard.questionText: What is 'write-ahead logging' (WAL) used for in databases?
options: Ensuring durability and atomicity by logging changes before applying them to the main data files
Caching write operations for batch execution

hard.questionText: What does the 'Halting Problem' prove about computation?
options: All programs can be proven to terminate with enough resources
It is undecidable whether an arbitrary program will halt or run forever on a given input
Non-deterministic programs are more powerful than deterministic ones

hard.questionText: What is the significance of the 'happens-before' relationship in Java's memory model?
options: It defines the order in which classes are loaded by the JVM
It guarantees that memory writes by one thread are visible to another thread in a defined order
It determines the order of garbage collection cycles
It specifies the sequence of static initializer blocks

hard.questionText: Which of the following describes 'phantom reads' in database transaction isolation?
options: Reading data that has been deleted mid-transaction
A transaction re-executing a query finds new rows that were inserted by another committed transaction
Seeing uncommitted changes from another transaction
Reading the same row twice and getting different values

hard.questionText: What is 'monadic composition' in functional programming?
options: Composing pure functions with no context
Chaining operations that return values wrapped in a context (like Maybe or IO) using bind/flatMap
Combining several microservices into a monolith

hard.questionText: In the context of compilers, what is 'register allocation'?
options: Assigning network ports to running processes
The process of mapping program variables to a limited number of CPU registers to optimize performance
Allocating heap memory for program objects
Tracking function call stacks at runtime

hard.questionText: What is the primary purpose of a 'bloom filter'?
options: To probabilistically test whether an element is a member of a set with possible false positives but no false negatives
To detect duplicate values in a stream with perfect accuracy

hard.questionText: What does 'copy-on-write' (COW) semantics mean in operating systems and data structures?
options: Data is always duplicated when assigned to a new variable
A shared resource is copied only when a modification is made, avoiding unnecessary duplication
All writes are buffered until the program exits
Data is written to disk before being modified in memory

hard.questionText: In language theory, what distinguishes a 'context-free grammar' from a 'regular grammar'?
options: Regular grammars support recursion; context-free grammars do not
Context-free grammars can describe nested and recursive structures that regular grammars cannot
Context-free grammars are parsed faster than regular grammars
Regular grammars require more memory to parse

hard.questionText: What is 'software transactional memory' (STM)?
options: Persisting transactions to an in-memory database
A concurrency control mechanism that allows groups of memory operations to execute atomically like database transactions
A cache strategy for database query results
A garbage collection algorithm for transient objects

hard.questionText: What distinguishes an 'epoll' from a 'select' system call in Linux I/O multiplexing?
options: select supports more file descriptors than epoll
epoll scales in O(1) for event notification regardless of the number of monitored descriptors, while select scales in O(n)
epoll is only available on Windows; select works on Linux
select uses edge-triggering; epoll uses level-triggering exclusively

hard.questionText: What is 'escape analysis' in JVM optimization?
options: Determining whether an object's reference escapes a method or thread, allowing it to be stack-allocated instead of heap-allocated
Identifying memory leaks in long-running applications
Analyzing exception propagation paths in bytecode

hard.questionText: What is the 'diamond problem' in multiple inheritance and how does Python resolve it?
options: A naming conflict in interfaces resolved by using abstract classes
Ambiguity when a class inherits from two classes sharing a common ancestor; Python resolves it via the C3 linearization MRO algorithm
A circular import issue resolved by lazy loading
A deadlock scenario in threaded programs resolved by lock ordering

hard.questionText: What is 'ahead-of-time (AOT) compilation' compared to 'just-in-time (JIT) compilation'?
options: AOT compiles code during execution; JIT compiles before deployment
AOT translates code to machine code before runtime; JIT compiles code during execution for optimization
AOT is used only for scripting languages; JIT only for compiled languages
They are identical in performance characteristics

hard.questionText: In distributed systems, what does the 'SAGA pattern' address?
options: Managing long-running distributed transactions by breaking them into local transactions with compensating rollback actions
Replicating leader state to follower nodes
Batching writes to reduce network overhead

hard.questionText: What is 'continuation-passing style' (CPS) in programming?
options: A style where every function logs its execution trace
A programming style where control flow is passed explicitly as a 'continuation' callback argument instead of using return values
A method of chaining Promises in JavaScript
An approach to writing tail-recursive functions in Haskell

hard.questionText: What is 'consistent hashing' and why is it used in distributed systems?
options: A cryptographic hash used for authentication tokens
A technique that minimizes the remapping of keys when nodes are added or removed from a distributed cache or cluster
A method to ensure all hash functions return the same result
A way to shard databases using sequential integer IDs

hard.questionText: What is the difference between 'strong' and 'weak' references in memory management?
options: Strong references are faster to dereference than weak ones
Strong references prevent garbage collection of the referenced object; weak references allow the GC to collect it even if a reference exists
Weak references are used only for primitive types
Strong references exist on the stack; weak references exist on the heap

hard.questionText: What is 'algebraic data type' (ADT) in functional programming?
options: A numeric type with extended precision for algebraic computations
A composite type formed by combining other types using sum types (variants) and product types (records)
A class hierarchy in Scala that enforces mathematical laws
A data structure that supports algebraic operations like addition and multiplication

hard.questionText: What is the purpose of 'vector clocks' in distributed systems?
options: To synchronize wall-clock time across nodes
To track causality and partial ordering of events across distributed processes without relying on a global clock
To measure latency between distributed nodes
To schedule tasks based on priority across a cluster

hard.questionText: What does 'trampolining' solve in recursive programming?
options: It speeds up tree traversal algorithms
It prevents stack overflow in deeply recursive functions by converting recursion into an iterative loop using thunks
It optimizes tail calls at the hardware level
It allows mutual recursion in languages without first-class functions

hard.questionText: What is 'data-oriented design' (DOD) and how does it differ from OOP?
options: DOD uses inheritance extensively while OOP avoids it
DOD organizes data in memory layouts optimized for CPU cache efficiency, prioritizing data transformation over object behavior encapsulation
DOD is a database design paradigm while OOP is for application logic
DOD enforces stricter type safety than OOP through dependent types

hard.questionText: What is the difference between 'optimistic' and 'pessimistic' concurrency control in databases?
options: Optimistic locks records immediately; pessimistic assumes no conflicts and checks only at commit
Optimistic assumes no conflicts and validates at commit; pessimistic locks resources upfront to prevent conflicts
They are identical strategies with different naming conventions
Pessimistic concurrency is only used in NoSQL databases

hard.questionText: What is 'partial application' in functional programming and how does it differ from currying?
options: They are identical; both fully decompose functions into unary functions
Partial application fixes some arguments of a function returning a new function with fewer args; currying transforms an n-ary function into a chain of n unary functions
Currying fixes only the first argument; partial application fixes all but one
Partial application only works on pure functions; currying works on any function

hard.questionText: In CPU architecture, what is 'branch prediction' and why does it matter for performance?
options: It preloads data into L1 cache before it is needed
It is the CPU's mechanism to guess the outcome of conditional branches to keep the pipeline full, avoiding costly pipeline flushes on mispredictions
It predicts the next memory address to fetch from RAM
It schedules threads across cores to minimize context switching

hard.questionText: What is 'higher-kinded types' (HKT) in type theory and which mainstream language supports them natively?
options: Types parameterized by other parameterized types (type constructors); Scala and Haskell support them natively
Types that inherit from multiple interfaces; supported in Java and C#
Types with runtime reflection capabilities; supported in Python and Ruby
Generic types constrained by numeric operations; supported in TypeScript and Rust

hard.questionText: What is the 'LMAX Disruptor' pattern and what problem does it solve?
options: A NoSQL storage pattern for reducing write latency in document databases
A high-performance inter-thread messaging library using a ring buffer to eliminate lock contention and enable millions of transactions per second
A design pattern for disrupting legacy monolithic architectures with microservices
A garbage collection algorithm that reduces pause times in JVM applications

hard.questionText: What is 'bidirectional type checking' in modern type systems?
options: Type checking that validates both input and output of every function simultaneously
A type inference algorithm that alternates between checking (propagating type info inward) and synthesis (inferring types outward) to reduce required annotations
A method for checking types both at compile time and runtime
Validating that two modules export matching type signatures

hard.questionText: In the context of garbage collection, what is the 'tri-color marking' algorithm?
options: A compression algorithm that categorizes heap objects by size into three buckets
A concurrent GC algorithm that classifies objects as white (unvisited), gray (discovered but not fully scanned), and black (fully scanned) to identify live objects without stopping the world
A generational GC strategy dividing the heap into young, middle-aged, and old generations
A reference counting scheme using three bits to track object lifetimes

hard.questionText: What is 'row-oriented' versus 'column-oriented' storage in databases and when is each preferred?
options: Row-oriented stores rows contiguously, ideal for OLAP analytics; column-oriented stores columns contiguously, ideal for OLTP transactions
Row-oriented stores rows contiguously, ideal for OLTP transactional workloads; column-oriented stores columns contiguously, ideal for OLAP analytical queries over many rows
They are equivalent in performance for all workloads
Column-oriented storage is only used for in-memory databases

hard.questionText: What is 'supercompilation' in compiler optimization theory?
options: Parallel compilation of source files across multiple CPU cores
An aggressive optimization technique that symbolically evaluates a program and specializes it by propagating information about inputs throughout the entire call graph
A method for compiling multiple languages into a single binary
Ahead-of-time compilation with profile-guided optimization enabled

hard.questionText: What is the 'Reactive Manifesto' and what four traits does it define for reactive systems?
options: A testing methodology defining unit, integration, system, and acceptance tests
A design philosophy defining that reactive systems should be Responsive, Resilient, Elastic, and Message-Driven
An API design standard defining stateless, cacheable, layered, and uniform interfaces
A microservices architecture defining isolated, independently deployable, auto-scaling, and observable services
